name: Torii
heading: A Zero Trust eBPF Powered E2EE Gateway.
description: I am currently in the process of developing torii for my master's capstone applied research project. Torii is effectively the modern take on a reverse proxy, built in Rust with a central focus of zero trust. The core difference between it and other proxies is opinionated defaults and new features. Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), wildcard certificates with certificate renewal, end-to-end encryption (E2EE) using lightweight application sidecars, and an eBPF XDP bouncer.
This is not a strict list of all included or supported features.

name: Home Server
heading: Self-hosted Cloud.
description: During my senior year of high school, I started tinkering with a 2010 Mac mini. I loaded a standard 22.04 Ubuntu install onto it and started the hobby of self-hosting, mainly game servers for Minecraft. During the spring of 2023, my second semester of college, I bought everything a gaming computer would require except a GPU. Thus, it became my first powerhouse of a server with a 5700G, 64GB of DDR4 RAM, and a 2TB 980 Pro NVMe SSD running Ubuntu Server 22.04 LTS. I avoided AM5 at the time, as I believed I might face instability with the new platform.

This powerhouse is where I learned a vast majority of Linux, Docker, and the various self-hosted tooling that are available. The headless environment forced me to grow comfortable with the CLI and enabled me to realize the actual capabilities of a server. Having built a home server, I have learned an incredible amount that has assisted me throughout my college experience, courses, and life. I've slowly refined my understanding of how computers from anyone's house to the cloud operate.

Now three years into running this, I decided my desktop rig and server were overdue for an upgrade. I shifted my desktop to AM5 and moved my 5700X3D to my server, repurposing my old RTX 3060 for media transcoding and local AI. During the three years, I have also purchased a 20TB and 26TB manufacture-recertified HDD around January 2024 and 2025, respectively. The intention of these is media and cloud storage in comparison to cloud-hosting options. Comically, in 2026, as of writing this, the same HDDs are significantly more expensive.

When I upgraded my server in the spring of 2026, I had planned to do a nuke and pave, as I called it. Once the spring semester was over for my graduate studies, I spent a week ensuring my backups were intact in the cloud, took a local copy, and committed to a major release upgrade of the OS with a clean install. I decided to swap to Ubuntu Server 26.04 as it was published in the spring during my studies, and I'd learned of some nice features present. Mainly sudo-rs instead of sudo and Incus being published in the apt repository.

Rebuilding the services on an updated OS I learned how to better structure my files for backups, properly back up my databases as dumps instead of live, check the integrity of my Kopia backups, and harden my Compose files while shifting them to Git. Stating these three after the fact is simple, but the planning and validation process took quite a bit of time. Months later I am still running with an uptime of 99-100%, and my system is incredibly low-maintenance. For those in the self-hosted hobby, this is something you only learn to appreciate over time. Tinkering with my stack is fun, but after a bit it's nice to have functional services that take care of themselves.

name: AI-Powered C2 Server
heading: An AI-driven Command & Control infrastructure server.
description: I co-developed in a group of eight for my graduate software engineering semester project. We created an AI-powered C2 server, and a majority of my development was focused on the backend. I developed a scalable, high-performance payload management pipeline. Featuring a concurrent Rust-based payload factory utilizing semaphores to manage build-job throttling safely and effectively.

Jobs are orchestrated via Redis queues, with background workers for processing tasks efficiently. API endpoints handle secure distribution, backed by PostgreSQL for state persistence and immutable audit logging. Automated filesystem cleanup routines were engineered to remove stale build artifacts while preserving database integrity for historical tracking.
Mirrored code from GitHub organization of the PayloadFactory and TeamServerAPI is avaliable on my self-hosted forgejo.

name: Ember
heading: A cloud-native code snippet manager.
description: During the fall semester of 2025 at college, I took a graduate course in cloud computing. This is one of the two graduate courses I had the opportunity to participate in during this semester. Part of the class was a final project that needed to use some form of cloud technologies. I came up with the idea for a code snippet manager after deploying the self-hosted tool ByteStash.

I sought to create a different version of that tool with some expanded features and better visual safety. One standout feature Ember offers is the ability to rapidly create a snippet and have its cyclomatic complexity computed. Complexity calculation is performed in merely seconds after a user submits a new snippet, and all snippet interactions are logged. I developed Ember using Nuxt 4, having started a project, still in progress, to learn about the framework after hearing about it during the summer of 2025. Having completed a previous project in Vue, I was compelled to use Nuxt, as it offers a full stack. It's got some incredible features and helped me learn a tremendous amount about what is possible in web development. The code is open-source, unlike my other project for school, as I created the application myself, and I intended to carry it forward, as I think it's a resourceful tool with incredible potential.

name: Dota-2-Stats
heading: A game statistic web application for Dota 2.
description: For my spring semester at college in 2025 I took Software Engineering. The emphasis of this class was making software with a group project being the culmination of the semester's effort. I worked in a group of six and specifically my role was the backend / data integration. I had presented the project idea as well so I was the product owner. We used scrum methodology and developed over one week sprints. I created the hooks for the API we used and later integrated a database and proper backend with express.js. This was to cache data fetched and reduce loading times. It proved a challenge but I got to learn a lot especially developing my Vue skills and experimenting with MongoDB and its data processing. The code is private as the software is school owned, and a live demo isn't available publicly because it exposes the backend processes. At request a demo can be demonstrated.

name: My Portfolio
heading: My personal portfolio website.
description: This site is a portfolio showcasing my work and skills. It is built with Vue and TailwindCSS. It is meant to be the central hub of all things regarding me and my professional career. It's also a passion project and a blog for what I'm up to in my life.
